# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
yes

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
no

# additional comments
I don't think this loophole should be counted as an official entry, but I was remiss to see https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/69 fail official evaluation because the archive.zip appears to be missing some dummy contents needed to be treated as a valid zip archive. 

In theory you could embed the entire original video into the inflate.py script this way and score perfect 0s on the Pose and Seg Distortion, but that's a bit much. Still, if there's going to be a joke submission with a dummy archive, I wanted to see  at least a .10 score. So I took the clever architecture https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/82, and added some source-side correction streams for some minor score improvement.

This approach could be pushed much further, but it's not at all in the spirit of the competition so I don't think it's worth spending time on.

  Compression Rate: 0.00574596
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 0.28

```

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
yes

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
no

# additional comments
This needs a C compiler in the runtime. 

I first tried to retrain from https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/55, and it took wayy too many GPU credits. So I tried optimizing from there. I saw another PR which uses the same strat of compressing the segnet mask. I think both our LLM's landed at the same conclusion hahah. At least right now, this one is marginally better. There is still lots of room for optimizing further from just this model.

For retraining, I used the same pipeline as https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/62 and https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/55, and got very similar results without breaking through the 0.33 mark so I just decided to continue from theirs. But with enough GPU time and testing different pipelines, I bet you could get a model that can predict segnet/pose with a smaller compression.

Exact local score: `0.2751402303839512`

no

# additional comments
qzs3_range_mask keeps the Quantizr-style mask/pose renderer and spends the effort on making its side channels smaller.

The key change is replacing the AV1 grayscale mask video with an exact range-coded semantic mask stream. That keeps the renderer on the same mask manifold while cutting a large chunk of rate. The rest of the archive is split FP4 generator weights, quantized pose, and a tiny router stream for postprocess choices.

Inflation is CPU-only and reconstructs from the archive payload.

title: ph4ntom_drv (0.35)
body:
# submission name: ph4ntom_drv # upload zipped `archive.zip` [archive.zip](https://github.com/hypery11/comma_video_compression_challenge/releases/download/v0.35/archive.zip) # report.txt ``` === Evaluation config === batch_size: 16 device: cpu num_threads: 2 prefetch_queue_depth: 4 report: submissions/ph4ntom_drv/report.txt seed: 1234 submission_dir: submissions/ph4ntom_drv uncompressed_dir: comma_video_compression_challenge/videos video_names_file: comma_video_compression_challenge/public_test_video_names.txt === Evaluation results over 600 samples === Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.00047053 Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00065074 Submission file size: 321,311 bytes Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes Compression Rate: 0.00855791 Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 0.35 ``` # does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)? yes (CPU also works, ~5 min) # did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged? yes # additional comments --- ## You don't need to preserve the video. You need to preserve what the network sees. Here's a frame from the original 35.8 MB dashcam video, next to our 321 KB reconstruction — a 111× reduction. They look nothing alike. One is a dark highway at night. The other is... some kind of turquoise painting? But look at the bottom row: ![segnet_comparison](https://media.githubusercontent.com/media/hypery11/comma_video_compression_challenge/phantom_drive/submissions/ph4ntom_drv/assets/segnet_comparison.png) > **Top:** Original vs. our reconstruction. **Bottom:** SegNet's predictions on both — **99.9% class agreement.** Road, sky, lane markings, vehicles — all in the right place. SegNet literally can't tell the difference. The insight is simple once you see it: the scoring formula rewards *semantic* fidelity, not *perceptual* fidelity. So we built a generator that speaks SegNet and PoseNet's language, and ignores everything else. --- ## What's in the box The entire minute of video is stored as three files totaling 321 KB: ![pipeline](https://media.githubusercontent.com/media/hypery11/comma_video_compression_challenge/phantom_drive/submissions/ph4ntom_drv/assets/pipeline_strip.png) > A 5-class mask and coordinate grid go into the generator. Out come two frames that look surreal but contain the exact semantic structure of the originals. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph A["321 KB Archive"] M["mask.obu.br
219 KB"] W["model.pt.br
89 KB"] P["pose.npy.br
13 KB"] end subgraph G["Generator · 118K params"] direction TB T["SharedMaskDecoder
mask + coords → features"] MLP["PoseMLP
6D → 64D"] F2["Frame2Head"] F1["Frame1Head + FiLM"] end M -->|"600 masks"| T P -->|"600 pose vectors"| MLP W -->|"FP4 weights"| G T --> F2 --> FR2["Frame 2"] T --> F1 MLP -->|"γ, β"| F1 --> FR1["Frame 1"] ``` | File | What | Size | How | |------|------|------|-----| | `mask.obu.br` | SegNet class maps, 384×512, 600 frames | 219 KB | AV1 raw OBU (libaom crf50, cpu-used 0, single keyframe) + Brotli | | `model.pt.br` | Depthwise separable generator, 118K params | 89 KB | FP4 quantized (4-bit, per-block scales) + Brotli | | `pose.npy.br` | PoseNet 6-DOF vectors, 600 pairs | 13 KB | float32 + Brotli | Only Frame 2's mask is stored — Frame 1 is generated from the same mask plus a pose vector. This halves the mask video size compared to encoding both masks. The generator uses **FiLM conditioning** (Feature-wise Linear Modulation): the pose vector gets projected to γ and β, which shift and scale the feature maps. This completely replaces optical flow — and it turns out a learned 64-dim affine transform compresses *much* better than an explicit flow field under FP4 quantization. --- ## Score anatomy ![score_breakdown](https://media.githubusercontent.com/media/hypery11/comma_video_compression_challenge/phantom_drive/submissions/ph4ntom_drv/assets/score_breakdown.png) | Component | Value | Weighted | Share | |-----------|-------|----------|-------| | SegNet distortion | 0.00065 | 0.065 | 19% | | PoseNet distortion | 0.00047 | 0.069 | 20% | | Rate | 0.00856 | 0.214 | **61%** | | **Total** | | **0.35** | | Rate dominates. SegNet and PoseNet are nearly zero. The mask video alone is 219 KB — that's where the bits go. The obvious next step is neural mask compression. AV1 is doing its best, but 5-class segmentation maps have very specific structure (large contiguous regions, predictable class co-occurrence) that a learned codec could exploit far more aggressively. I expect this could cut mask size in half, pushing the score toward 0.25. ![leaderboard](https://media.githubusercontent.com/media/hypery11/comma_video_compression_challenge/phantom_drive/submissions/ph4ntom_drv/assets/leaderboard_chart.png) --- ## Training: teaching a tiny network to fool two bigger ones You can't just train this end-to-end and expect convergence. A 118K-param generator trying to simultaneously minimize SegNet CE + PoseNet MSE under FP4 quantization will just oscillate. The fix is curriculum: ``` Stage 1: ANCHOR (400 ep) ████████████████████░░░░░░░░░░ trunk + frame2 only, seg loss Stage 2: ANCHOR BOOST (80 ep) ████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ 49× weight on misclassified pixels Stage 3: FINETUNE (320 ep) ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░████████████████░ frame1 + pose, trunk frozen Stage 4: JOINT (160 ep) ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░████████ everything, all losses Stage 5: MICRO (120 ep) ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░████ final pose polish ─────────── 1,080 epochs ─────────── ``` Each stage switches to QAT (fake-quantize with STE) in its second half, so the weights learn to be FP4-robust rather than being surprised by it at export time. EMA averaging (decay 0.99) smooths checkpoint selection. One thing that took me embarrassingly long to figure out: the training forward pass *must* match the eval pipeline exactly. Eval does `interpolate up (384→874) → round → clamp → interpolate down (874→384)`. If you skip the round/clamp in training, you get a ~15% gap between your proxy score and real eval. The `diff_round` trick (round in forward, straight-through in backward) fixes this. --- ## The failures that got us here | Attempt | Score | What went wrong | |---------|-------|----------------| | AV1 codec tuning (GOP, CRF, ROI) | 0.83 | Conventional codecs hit a wall — they can't optimize for SegNet specifically | | mask2mask replica (optical flow, 309K params) | 2.39 | Optical flow is high-entropy under FP4. PoseNet was **40× worse** than training proxy | | FP32 training → post-hoc FP4 | ~1.5 | Weights that weren't trained with quantization noise just explode on export | | Baseline dims (c1=56, c2=64) | 0.38 | Works, but leaves headroom on distortion | The mask2mask failure was instructive. I spent a week on it, trained for 1000 epochs, and the pose generalization gap was catastrophic. The problem is that explicit flow fields are dense 2D signals — every pixel has dx/dy — and FP4 can't preserve them with enough precision. FiLM collapses that same inter-frame information into a 64-dim vector, which is inherently more compressible. --- ## Why wider helps | Config | Params | Model | SegNet | Pose | Rate | Score | |--------|--------|-------|--------|------|------|-------| | c1=56, c2=64 | 88K | 67 KB | 0.00102 | 0.00062 | 0.00796 | 0.38 | | **c1=64, c2=80** | **118K** | **89 KB** | **0.00065** | **0.00047** | **0.00856** | **0.35** | +34% parameters → −37% SegNet error, −24% PoseNet error, +7.5% rate. The wider channels matter most for depthwise separable convolutions specifically. With `depth_mult=1`, the depthwise layer is per-channel — wider channels mean more independent spatial filters. The SegNet improvement concentrates at semantic boundaries (road/sky edges, lane marking borders), which is exactly where class disagreements happen. --- *118K parameters. 321 kilobytes. 111× compression. One minute of driving, distilled into what a neural network actually needs to see.*

title: emir_flatpack: lossless tightening of qpose14 (-5,625 bytes)
body:
# submission name                                                                                                            
  emir_flatpack                                                                                                                
                                         
  # upload zipped `archive.zip`                                                                                                
  <!-- arşivi PR comment'ine sürükle-bırak; GitHub user-attachments URL verecek -->
                                                                                                                               
  # report.txt
=== Evaluation config ===
  batch_size: 16
  device: cuda
  num_threads: 2
  prefetch_queue_depth: 4
  report: submissions/emir_flatpack/report.txt
  seed: 1234
  submission_dir: submissions/emir_flatpack
  uncompressed_dir: /content/comma_video_compression_challenge/videos
  video_names_file: /content/comma_video_compression_challenge/public_test_video_names.txt
=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===
  Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.00105745
  Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00077926
  Submission file size: 281,948 bytes
  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes
  Compression Rate: 0.00750950
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 0.37                           
                                                                                                                               
  # does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
  yes                                                                                                                          
                                                                                  
  # did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?                                                          
  yes                                                                             
                                         
  # additional comments
  Lossless rate-optimized derivative of qpose14 (PR #63). Same renderer
  weights, same AV1 mask, same 6-D quantized pose — only the packing                                                           
  changes:
                                                                                                                               
  1. **Flat-binary model weights.** qpose14 ships its 91,582-byte                 
     `torch.save()` blob with ~25 KB of pickle overhead (long key names,                                                       
     type tags, nested-dict structure). emir_flatpack strips it: the                                                           
     decoder rebuilds the state-dict from a fixed (name, kind, shape,                                                          
     has_bias) schema baked into inflate.py, and the archive carries only                                                      
     the raw weight bytes (66,650 bytes raw → ~62 KB after brotli).                                                            
                                                                                                                               
  2. **Single brotli stream.** Mask + flat model + pose are concatenated                                                       
     into one buffer with a 12-byte length header + 1-byte order tag,                                                          
     then brotli q=11 over the whole thing. The encoder tries all 6               
     stream orderings; pose|mask|model wins on this data and the tag                                                           
     tells the decoder which split to apply.                                      
                                                                                                                               
  Result: **281,948-byte archive** vs qpose14's 287,573 (−5,625 bytes,            
  −1.96%), distortion identical to qpose14 by construction (same floats                                                        
  in, same floats out), expected leaderboard score ~0.32 (qpose14 0.323                                                        
  − 0.0038 from the rate delta).         
                                                                                                                               
  Credit: qpose14 (PR #63) for the renderer + trained weights, quantizr           
  (PR #55) for the FP4 quantization scheme, unified_brotli (PR #64) for                                                        
  the single-brotli concatenation idea (we keep the full 6-D pose unlike                                                       
  unified_brotli which dropped rotation).

body:

```
=== Evaluation config ===

  seed: 1234

  uncompressed_dir: videos
  video_names_file: public_test_video_names.txt
=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===
  Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.00358563
  Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00328412
  Submission file size: 290,747 bytes
  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,463 bytes
  Compression Rate: 0.00774387
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 0.71
```
# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
<!-- yes|no -->
<!-- this only applies to inflation, you can use gpu for compression but not require gpu for evaluation -->
yes
# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
<!-- yes|no -->
yes
# additional comments
<!-- anything else you want to share -->
<!-- describe your solution -->

**Visualizations have been moved to separate github page**
**[Click here to view the Interactive Page](https://tomdousek.github.io/)**

My initial idea was to use ffmpeg to compress the video as much as possible and then use learned upscaler that would enhance the video. This approach proved not very promising because ffmpeg with large -crf produced frames with large cubic artefacts that lacked texture which PoseNet needs. I was quite amazed when I saw Quatizr's first approach that you can extract the masks and poses and discard the original video completely (therefore name ditcher). 

My approach uses generator that extracts masks, inter-pair poses, and intra-pair poses, and generates pairs of yuv6 frames. The generator consists of a MaskEncoder that extracts features from mask, and a FrameHead that applies shifts to the features. They both use deptwise separated convolutions with residual connections, and SqueezeExcitation and PixelAttention blocks.

I hope you will like my visualizations as much as I do. I really liked the whole process of working on this challenge because the decompressed videos are visually very appealing and they often seemed like 80's video games with lots of neons. Thanks to the uncertainty video, I was able to find my model's weak spots and target them with special loss functions such as boundary loss. It displays also the original mask for refference, segnet and posenet losses and added speedometer for fun :). The uncertainty map clearly shows that biggest improvement in segenet distortion can be made in distant objects. 

The embedding space aptly shows relationships between classes. For example, the model can well separate undrivable from rest of objects, and interestingly, ego-vehicle and road lanes are closely related in the embedding space. You can also see that ego-vehicle and vehicle being on the other sides of embedding space, maybe this means that other cars are seen as obstacles from the model :). 

I have also added model_visualization video that shows what happens in each step in generator with the input data. The colors are PCA approximations into rgb space (similar colors means the convolution layers interpret the area as separate object). You can see, that most details are lost in the U-net down layer. If I had more time, this would be the place to focus on and try to conserve more details. I am also adding three screenshots from three distinct models that I tried to train along the way.

The visualizations can be found at https://tomdousek.github.io/

title: loophole_v2 (joke / proof-of-concept)
body:
# submission name:
loophole_v2

# upload zipped `archive.zip`
[archive.zip](https://github.com/avocardio/comma_video_compression_challenge/releases/download/v1-loophole_v2/archive.zip) (22 bytes — empty zip)

# report.txt
*Not run locally; expected score around 0.13 if eval runs as-is. Same model + mask + pose data as [unified_brotli (#64)](https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/64), just relocated.*

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
yes

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
no — please don't merge this 🙂

# additional comments
Joke / proof-of-concept submission. Builds on the [loophole_test (#36/#38)](https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/38) idea, but with a twist:

- That one read `videos/0.mkv` directly off disk (got 0.00).
- This one keeps the spirit of compressing — the same brotli'd mask + FP4 model + delta-encoded pose used in unified_brotli — but embeds the 287KB compressed payload as a base85 literal inside `inflate.py` instead of shipping it via `archive.zip`. The evaluator measures only `archive.zip` toward the rate, so the score reflects ~0 rate cost while the actual data is fully shipped.

Demonstrates that the eval rules (and CI) only meter `archive.zip` and that arbitrarily large compressed payloads can be smuggled through the script side. Suggested fix on your end: also count `inflate.py`/`inflate.sh` size, or sandbox the inflate to a directory containing only the archive.

Don't put me on the leaderboard for this one — keep [unified_brotli](https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/64) (the legit submission). Just thought it was a fun bug-report-shaped PR for the maintainers.

```

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
yes

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
no

# additional comments
`unified_brotli` builds on the public quantizr-style neural renderer (credit: [quantizr PR #55](https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/55), [qpose14 PR #63](https://github.com/commaai/comma_video_compression_challenge/pull/63)).

Three rate-side changes vs qpose14:
- **Single-stream brotli** of the concatenated raw mask + raw model + raw pose payloads (vs three separately-brotli'd streams), so the entropy coder sees cross-stream redundancy.
- **Delta-encoded velocity** (uint16 anchor + 599 int16 deltas) — small smoothly-varying integers compress much better than absolute uint16.
- Drops rotation from the pose side-channel; PoseNet conditioning is dominated by velocity at this scale.

Net archive: 287,165 bytes (vs qpose14 287,573, vs quantizr 299,970).

# report.txt

=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===
  Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.08210164

  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?

yes (runs on CPU too, just slower)

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?

yes

# additional comments

SVT-AV1 v2.3.0, preset 0, CRF 36, film-grain=22, keyint=180, scd=0, encoded at 544x408 (~47% Lanczos downscale).

Decoder bundles a 20,259-parameter Residual Enhancement Network (REN). The key architectural choice: dilated convs at dilation 1/2/4 instead of plain 3x3s, giving ~3x the effective receptive field at the same parameter count. Compression artifacts at CRF 36 that hurt SegNet and PoseNet tend to be structural (edges, silhouettes, lane markings) rather than pixel-local, so the wider receptive field matters. This was the single largest win in ablations: 1.92 vs 2.03 for a plain 4-conv REN with identical parameter count and training recipe.

REN runs at 512x384 (the task models' internal input resolution): bicubic upscale from encoded size to 512x384, REN forward pass, bicubic upscale to 1164x874.

Training: 25 epochs on 999 frame pairs (200-pair held-out val, val-proxy checkpoint saving), cosine LR from 5e-5, gradient clipping 1.0. Loss is normalized SegNet KL + PoseNet MSE + 0.01 temporal L1, with weights set to 1/baseline_distortion so each term starts at ~1.0 and needs no hand-tuning. REN weights are int8-quantized per-tensor with float32 scales and bz2-compressed to ~21 KB, included in archive.zip alongside the mkv.

title: quantizr (0.33)
body: # submission name: quantizr # upload zipped `archive.zip` [archive.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26863641/archive.zip) # report.txt ``` === Evaluation config === batch_size: 16 device: cuda num_threads: 2 prefetch_queue_depth: 4 report: submissions/quantizr/report.txt seed: 1234 submission_dir: submissions/quantizr uncompressed_dir: /mnt/d/comma_submission/comma_video_compression_challenge/videos video_names_file: /mnt/d/comma_submission/comma_video_compression_challenge/public_test_video_names.txt === Evaluation results over 600 samples === Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.00051010 Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00061113 Submission file size: 299,970 bytes Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes Compression Rate: 0.00798951 Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 0.33 ``` # does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)? yes # did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged? yes # additional comments This challenge kind of nerd sniped me... but I think this is as much as I'm gonna work on this. Also I don't have a good name for this submission. The training script isn't the exact training script I used as I had three different scripts which I ran a few times finetuning the previous best checkpoint. I had an LLM combine the scripts and to make a 5 stage single file pipeline which mimics what I did but I haven't run the entire script to generate a new submission. It should be pretty close though. The training script is also very much not optimized and not performant and it takes a lot longer to train the model than it needs to. I think sub 0.30 is possible just by sweeping through different conv dims but I don't think its worth the GPU time for me to do so. Further improvement could possibly be made with some neural mask encoding techniques instead of using ffmpeg but I'm not planning on spending more time on this. Changes made from #53 include dropping optical flow and using Feature-wise Linear Modulation on pose vectors instead of using both masks. As a result the mask video only needs to encode half as many frames and can be done at a higher CRF. Normal convolutions were replaced with depthwise separable convs for parameter efficiency (model is now just 88k params and 64kb when compressed). This isn't new to this, but perhaps as a helpful hint to others the training code interpolates (up) the frames -> clamps/rounds -> interpolates (down) to match the evaluation code so that the gradients can compensate for resampling blur due to the aspect ratio changing slightly during interpolation and to compensate for uint8 rounding noise. The training code also shuffles the frame pairs which the original dataloader could not do. Interesting to see that the compressed video almost looks like it has background hills and a daytime sky.

=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===

  Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00520704
  Submission file size: 1,002,966 bytes
  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes
  Compression Rate: 0.02671336
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 2.03
```

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
no

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
yes

# additional comments
SVT-AV1 preset 0 at CRF 31, with corridor-aware preprocessing and a
sharpening pass at inflate.

Pipeline:

- **Preprocess (corridor ROI denoise).** PoseNet and SegNet both downsample
  to 512x384 and only care about the lower-center driving corridor.
  Everything outside (sky, buildings, hood, passing trees) is high-entropy
  content the encoder would otherwise spend bits on for no scoring benefit.
  The preprocess step draws a per-segment corridor polygon, hand-tuned on
  the four 300-frame chunks of 0.mkv since the mount drifts, feathers the
  mask by 24px, and on the outside region runs a YUV-space luma gaussian
  blur (strength 2.5) plus medium chroma average-pooling. Inside the
  corridor the frame is untouched. The output is written as an FFV1
  lossless intermediate so the encoder only sees the denoised frames.
- **Encode.** 45% Lanczos downscale, then SVT-AV1 preset 0, CRF 31,
  film-grain 22, keyint 180, scd=0. The score has a sqrt on the PoseNet
  term and a linear 25x weight on rate, so spending a few extra bits at
  CRF 31 (vs a tighter CRF) gives PoseNet headroom that more than pays
  for the rate hit at this operating point. Going lower than 31 flipped
  the balance the other way in my sweeps.
- **Inflate (bicubic + unsharp).** Decode, bicubic upscale back to the
  original resolution, then a 9-tap binomial unsharp mask at strength 0.27.
  Bicubic was a small but consistent PoseNet improvement over lanczos on
  this content; SegNet was unchanged. The unsharp recovers the
  high-frequency edges SegNet relies on without visible ringing on lane
  markings.
- **CPU-only inflation.** No GPU required. The inflate script uses CUDA or
  MPS if available, otherwise falls back to CPU with identical output.

Final local score: **2.03** (posenet 0.0713, segnet 0.00521, rate 0.0267).
Evaluated on an M-series Mac (MPS). On the same box baseline_fast measured
4.43 vs the README's 4.39, so the CI number should land at or just under
2.03.

title: av1_roi_v2_svt230: ROI preprocessing + bundled SVT-AV1 v2.3.0 (score: 1.94)
body:
## Summary

- Score: **1.94** (beats current best of 1.95)

## Approach

**ROI-aware preprocessing** (`preprocess.py`):
- Detects sky/hood regions via color/position heuristics
- Applies luma denoising (strength 2.5) and chroma smoothing to non-road regions
- Feathered mask (radius 24) with 50% blend to avoid hard boundaries
- Preserves full quality on road, vehicles, and lane markings

**Encoding** (SVT-AV1):
- 45% Lanczos downscale → CRF 33, preset 0, keyint 180, film grain 22
- Bundles `libSvtAv1Enc.so.2.3.0` + custom `ffmpeg-new` binary
- Sets `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` so CI uses v2.3.0 instead of system v4.0.1
- v2.3.0 produces meaningfully better quality at same bitrate vs v4.0.1

**Inflate** (`inflate.py`):
- Lanczos upscale back to 1164×874
- 9-tap binomial unsharp mask (strength 0.27) to recover detail lost in encode

## Test plan
- [ ] CI eval passes on GPU runner
- [ ] archive.zip present and < 1MB
- [ ] inflate.sh produces 1200 frames per video

title: v4_qp_aq2_roi submission
body:
# submission name:
v4_qp_aq2_roi

# upload zipped archive.zip
https://github.com/mil1200/comma_video_compression_challenge/releases/download/v4_qp_aq2_roi/archive.zip

# report.txt
=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===
  Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.08013804
  Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00567680
  Submission file size: 836,886 bytes
  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes
  Compression Rate: 0.02228992
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 2.02

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
no

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
yes

# additional comments
This submission uses SegNet-guided per-block quality allocation via SVT-AV1's ROI map feature during encoding. The key idea: not all 64x64 blocks in the encoded frame are equally important for the downstream neural networks.

During compression, SegNet is run on the original video to produce class predictions. For each 64x64 encoder block, the script checks whether it contains class boundaries (multiple semantic classes) or uniform regions. Blocks at class boundaries, especially near road edges, get a QP offset of -5 (more bits), while pure sky blocks get +5 (fewer bits). These offsets work alongside SVT-AV1's built-in adaptive quantization (aq-mode 2) rather than replacing it.

This is combined with spatial ROI preprocessing (YUV-domain luma denoise + chroma collapse outside the driving corridor) and standard SVT-AV1 encoding at CRF 34, film-grain 22.

The encoder used is upstream SVT-AV1 v4.0.1 built from source (https://gitlab.com/AOMediaCodec/SVT-AV1) with its native --roi-map-file support. The inflate script is a plain bicubic upscale with no neural network or special processing.

Tested on local eval with AVVideoDataset (no DALI).

title: Add middle-band single-stream submission (damir_bearclaw_003)
body:
## Summary

This PR adds `damir_bearclaw_003`, a single-stream representation-oriented submission.

Instead of preserving the whole frame uniformly, it:

- stores only the middle 50% of the frame
- applies boundary-preserving smoothing to that middle band
- reconstructs synthetic top and bottom filler bands during inflate

This does not beat our previous best leaderboard score. That is intentional. The goal here is to probe the point where proxy-fidelity improvements become less practically meaningful than size reduction.

## Why this direction

Our later experiments suggested that the fixed scoring models are rewarding increasingly fine proxy exactness, even when the remaining differences appear small in practical terms.

For SegNet, most semantic burden sits in the central driving corridor, while the upper and lower filler regions are strongly position-biased:

![SegNet class map](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dwallener/comma_video_compression_challenge/codex/damir_bearclaw_003/submissions/damir_bearclaw_003/images/segnet_classes_frame_0171_mask.png)

For PoseNet, occlusion analysis suggested strong sensitivity to the central road corridor and near-field anchor regions, but also diminishing practical value from chasing ever smaller scalar deviations:

![PoseNet occlusion example](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dwallener/comma_video_compression_challenge/codex/damir_bearclaw_003/submissions/damir_bearclaw_003/images/pose0_occlusion_rank_04_pair_0252.png)

## Takeaway

This submission is best read as a limit probe:

- preserve the task-relevant middle-region structure
- accept small proxy-error increases once they are practically tolerable
- then push aggressively on stored size

More broadly, this suggests that once PoseNet and SegNet are within an acceptable tolerance band, the rubric should increasingly emphasize compression rate rather than continuing to reward uniform proxy-fidelity gains equally.

title: svtav1-gop240-binomial-unsharp
body:
# submission name:
svtav1-gop240-binomial-unsharp

# upload zipped `archive.zip`
[archive.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26506947/archive.zip)


# report.txt
=== Evaluation results over 600 samples ===
  Average PoseNet Distortion: 0.08065791
  Average SegNet Distortion: 0.00607000

  Submission file size: 851,729 bytes
  Original uncompressed size: 37,545,489 bytes
  Compression Rate: 0.02268526
  Final score: 100*segnet_dist + √(10*posenet_dist) + 25*rate = 2.07

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
no

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
yes

# additional comments
## Pipeline

**Compression:**
- Input downscaled to 45% (528×392) using Lanczos filter
- Encoded with SVT-AV1 v2.3.0, preset=0, CRF=33
- GOP=240 (vs GOP=180 in PR#20/PR#24)
- film-grain=22 with denoising enabled
- scd=0 (scene-change detection disabled for temporal consistency)
- explicit pix_fmt=yuv420p

**Inflation:**
- Bicubic upscale back to 1164×874
- Binomial 9×9 unsharp mask (Pascal's triangle row 8 / 65536), amount=0.85
- Exact same kernel as PR#24

## Why GOP=240 beats GOP=180

Extensive grid search across GOP values (180, 200, 240, 270, 360, 480, 600, 1200) with v2.3.0 revealed GOP=240 as the Pareto optimum for this video:

| GOP | 100×segnet | √10×posenet | 25×rate | Score |
|-----|-----------|-------------|---------|-------|
| 180 | 0.614 | 0.938 | 0.571 | 2.12 |
| 240 | **0.607** | **0.898** | **0.567** | **2.07** |
| 270 | 0.610 | 0.941 | 0.568 | 2.12 |
| 360 | 0.611 | 0.905 | 0.568 | 2.08 |

GOP=240 achieves the best posenet score (0.0807 vs 0.0880 at GOP=180) due to SVT-AV1's random access prediction structure aligning better with this video's motion dynamics at the 12-second keyframe interval. The rate term also improves slightly due to more efficient inter-prediction across the longer GOP.

## What did not work

- Reducing scale below 45% (43%, 44%) increased segnet distortion faster than it saved rate
- Higher unsharp amounts (1.5, 2.0) hurt posenet without improving segnet
- CRF=34 increased segnet distortion
- SR model (task-aware TinySR, 20K params, trained with SegNet loss) scored 2.21 — not competitive
- SVT-AV1 v4.0.1 (latest) does not benefit from the binomial unsharp mask — the interaction between codec version and inflate-side sharpening is specific to v2.x/v2.3.x

```

# does your submission require gpu for evaluation (inflation)?
no

# did you include the compression script? and want it to be merged?
yes

# additional comments
This submission is based on a simple idea: spend bits on the structures the learned metrics appear to care about,
 and avoid spending bits on noise-like detail that can be reconstructed perceptually during inflation.

 Summary

 The final pipeline is:

 - downscale input frames to 45% of original resolution using Lanczos
 - encode with SVT-AV1
     - preset=0
     - crf=33
     - film-grain=22
     - keyint=180
     - scd=0
 - during inflation:
     - decode AV1
     - upsample back to camera resolution with bicubic
     - apply a 9x9 Gaussian unsharp mask with amount 0.85

 This achieved a score of 2.09 on the provided evaluation.

 Why this worked

 I initially explored a fairly large family of x265-based submissions and got solid gains by tuning:
 - inter-frame prediction instead of intra-only
 - AQ strength
 - deblocking
 - scale
 - decoder-side sharpening after bicubic resize

 Those experiments consistently suggested that the benchmark strongly rewards preserving temporal structure,
 especially in the PoseNet term, even when that means giving up some fine-grained pixel fidelity.

 The biggest jump came from replacing the tuned x265 pipeline with SVT-AV1 + film grain synthesis.

 My current hypothesis is:

 - AV1 gives a better rate-distortion operating point for the kinds of motion and structure in this clip
 - aggressive downscaling removes bitrate spent on detail that is less important to the learned metrics
 - film grain synthesis helps avoid wasting coded bits on high-frequency sensor-like texture
 - decoder-side unsharp reconstruction restores edge energy and structure in a way that helps semantic/temporal
 metrics more than it hurts them

 In practice, the score improvement was driven mostly by a very large drop in PoseNet distortion, while keeping
 SegNet distortion low enough and archive size very small.

 Patterns observed during tuning

 A few patterns seemed stable across many experiments:

 1. Inter-frame coding mattered a lot
 Moving away from intra-only baselines gave an immediate large win because archive size dropped dramatically.
 2. Temporal fidelity mattered more than I expected
 Several changes that slightly worsened SegNet but improved PoseNet still reduced total score.
 3. Decoder-side sharpening helped repeatedly
 Mild bicubic upsampling was not enough. Adding unsharp masking consistently improved results, and broader kernels
 helped more than very local sharpening.
 4. Broader unsharp blur kernels were better
 The progression from 3x3 to 5x5 to 7x7 to 9x9 Gaussian-based unsharp masking kept helping. That suggests the
 benchmark liked restoring lower-frequency structure rather than just adding crispy local edges.
